title: Nesterov Accelerated Gradient: Looking Ahead
concept: Nesterov Accelerated Gradient improves on momentum by 'peeking ahead' to where the accumulated velocity is about to carry the optimizer, and measuring the gradient there instead of at the current position — correcting course earlier and cutting down on overshoot.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What's the risk of momentum's built-up velocity if it gets too strong?

options.text: It can carry the optimizer flying past a good minimum — overshooting it
It makes every step exactly the same size forever

exercises.explain: Exactly! So what if the optimizer could catch that overshoot BEFORE fully committing to the step?

cards.title: Driving by only looking at your feet
cards.body: Regular momentum decides how hard to hit the gas based only on the slope right under its current position, then commits to a big velocity-powered step. It's like flooring the gas without checking what's just ahead on the road.

cards.title: Peek ahead, then correct
cards.body: Nesterov Accelerated Gradient (NAG) first takes a quick 'lookahead' jump using the current velocity, checks the slope AT that lookahead spot, and uses THAT slope to correct the real step — catching problems, like an upcoming overshoot, before fully committing.

cards.title: A cyclist who scouts the bend before leaning in
cards.body: A cyclist speeding downhill who glances ahead and spots a sharp curve can start braking or leaning early. A cyclist who only feels the road under their current tire finds out about the curve too late and has to overcorrect hard.

cards.title: Correcting before overshoot happens
cards.body: If momentum's velocity is about to carry the optimizer past the bottom of a valley and up the far slope, Nesterov's lookahead step lands on that far slope FIRST, feels the uphill pull immediately, and reins in the real step before the full overshoot happens.

cards.title: It's not a totally different move, just a smarter measurement
cards.body: You might think Nesterov is a completely different strategy from momentum. Really, it uses the exact same velocity-accumulation idea — the only change is WHERE the slope gets measured, at the lookahead point instead of the current point — and that's enough to noticeably cut down on overshoot and wasted steps.

cards.title: Momentum vs. Nesterov
cards.body: Where each one measures the slope:

left.text:
Measures slope at the current spot
Then commits to the full velocity step

right.text:
Peeks ahead to where velocity is heading
Measures slope THERE, corrects early

exercises.prompt: What does Nesterov Accelerated Gradient do differently from plain momentum?

options.text: It measures the slope at a 'lookahead' point — where the velocity is about to carry it — instead of at the current position
It ignores velocity completely and starts fresh every step
It always takes twice as many steps as momentum

exercises.explain: Right! That lookahead measurement is the entire trick — same velocity idea, smarter timing on where the slope gets checked.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Nesterov's lookahead step tends to reduce overshoot compared to plain momentum.
exercises.explain: True! By checking the slope ahead of where it's heading, Nesterov can rein in the step before a full overshoot happens.

exercises.sentence: Nesterov Accelerated Gradient 'peeks ___' to where momentum is about to carry it before correcting the real step.
exercises.explain: Peeking ahead to the lookahead point is exactly what lets Nesterov correct its course earlier than plain momentum.

exercises.prompt: Which description matches how Nesterov Accelerated Gradient corrects its course, versus plain momentum?

options.text: It jumps ahead using the current velocity first, checks the slope THERE, then adjusts the real step using that lookahead information
It measures the slope only at the starting position and never adjusts for velocity
It removes velocity entirely and behaves exactly like plain SGD

exercises.explain: Right — peeking ahead before committing is exactly what separates Nesterov from plain momentum.

exercises.prompt: A deep network's training run has: (1) a steep ravine early on, (2) a long flat plateau in the middle, and (3) a good minimum near the end that momentum keeps overshooting. Which optimizer best handles ALL three, and why?

options.text: Nesterov Accelerated Gradient — it keeps momentum's velocity to power through ravines and plateaus, but its lookahead correction catches the overshoot near the minimum that plain momentum misses
Plain SGD — its zig-zagging is actually ideal for all three situations
Full-batch gradient descent — reading the whole dataset each step fixes ravines, plateaus, and overshoot automatically
None of these optimizers can handle more than one type of terrain at a time

exercises.explain: Exactly! Nesterov keeps everything momentum does well — canceling zig-zag, powering through plateaus — while its lookahead trick catches overshoot earlier than plain momentum can. Next up: optimizers that go a step further, adjusting each individual weight's step size on the fly instead of using one shared step size for everything.
